level_labels.-1: Pro data center (active tax incentives, enterprise zones, major industry hubs)
level_labels.1: Light regulations (environmental review, AI ordinances, minor requirements)
level_labels.2: Moderate restrictions (pending legislation, proposed moratoriums, significant permitting)
level_labels.3: Significant active restrictions (density limits, zone bans, active moratoriums)
level_labels.4: Comprehensive restrictions (outright ban or prohibitive regulations)

type_labels.data_center: Physical data center construction or operation restrictions
type_labels.ai: Artificial intelligence deployment or use regulations
type_labels.energy: Energy use or grid impact restrictions
type_labels.water: Water use restrictions affecting data centers

41059.name: Umatilla County
41059.title: Oregon Enterprise Zone — Google Mega-Campus Hub
41059.description: Home to one of Google's largest data center campuses. Oregon's enterprise zone property tax exemptions, zero sales tax, and cheap Columbia River hydropower make Umatilla County one of the most data center-friendly jurisdictions in the U.S.
sources: Oregon Enterprise Zone program; Google Dalles Campus (2006-present)

19113.name: Linn County
19113.title: Iowa Data Center Tax Exemption Hub
19113.description: Iowa eliminated sales and use taxes on data center equipment in 2010. Linn County (Cedar Rapids) hosts major Microsoft and Google facilities. Iowa's 0% equipment sales tax, competitive energy costs, and central location make it a premier data center market.
sources: Iowa Code §423.3 data center exemption (2010); Microsoft & Google data centers, Cedar Rapids

45015.name: Berkeley County
45015.title: South Carolina Data Center Incentive Zone — Amazon/AWS Hub
45015.description: South Carolina offers some of the strongest data center incentives in the nation: 20-year sales tax exemptions on equipment and 20-year property tax abatements. Berkeley County hosts major Amazon/AWS facilities and has become one of the East Coast's fastest-growing data center markets.
sources: SC Code §12-6-3360 data center incentives; Amazon/AWS data centers, Berkeley County

32029.name: Storey County
32029.title: Nevada Incentives — Switch SUPERNAP Campus
32029.description: Storey County (Tahoe Reno Industrial Center) hosts Switch's massive SUPERNAP data center campus. Nevada offers no corporate income tax, no personal income tax, and significant sales/use tax abatements for data center operators.
sources: NRS §374.357 data center exemptions; Switch SUPERNAP campus, Tahoe Reno Industrial Center

31055.name: Douglas County
31055.title: Nebraska Advantage Data Center Act — Google Hub
31055.description: Nebraska's Advantage Data Center Act (LB 775) provides sales tax exemptions on equipment for qualifying data centers. Douglas County (Omaha) hosts major Google data center facilities and benefits from low energy costs and central location.
sources: Nebraska LB 775, Advantage Data Center Act; Google data center, Omaha

18057.name: Hamilton County
18057.title: Indiana Data Center Tax Exemption Hub
18057.description: Indiana's HEA 1450 (2014) provides a 100% sales tax exemption on data center equipment. Hamilton County (Fishers/Noblesville) is a fast-growing data center hub leveraging Indiana's incentives, central US location, and competitive power rates.
sources: Indiana HEA 1450 (2014), IC 6-2.5-5-59 data center exemption

41027.name: Hood River County
41027.title: High-Intensity Computing Facilities Ban
41027.description: Hood River County voted to ban new cryptocurrency mining and high-intensity computing facilities (2022), citing electric grid strain and energy concerns.
sources: Hood River County Commissioner vote, June 2022

53007.name: Chelan County
53007.title: PUD Moratorium on High-Intensity Computing
53007.description: Chelan County PUD (public utility district) imposed a moratorium on new cryptocurrency mining and high-density computing operations due to electric grid capacity constraints.
sources: Chelan County PUD Board resolution, 2019

53017.name: Douglas County
53017.title: PUD Moratorium on High-Intensity Computing
53017.description: Douglas County PUD imposed capacity restrictions and moratoriums on cryptocurrency mining and high-intensity computing operations due to grid constraints.
sources: Douglas County PUD Board resolution, 2019

51107.name: Loudoun County
51107.title: Data Center Overlay Zone Restrictions
51107.description: Loudoun County ('Data Center Alley') adopted new zoning rules restricting data center development in rural/western areas and imposing strict density and setback requirements in all zones.
sources: Loudoun County Zoning Ordinance Amendment, ZOAM 2019-0004, 2023

30053.name: Lincoln County
30053.title: Cryptocurrency/High-Intensity Computing Restrictions
30053.description: Lincoln County passed ordinances restricting cryptocurrency mining and high-intensity computing operations, citing noise, energy, and land-use concerns.
sources: Lincoln County Commissioner ordinance, 2018

36019.name: Clinton County
36019.title: Plattsburgh Cryptocurrency Mining Moratorium
36019.description: The City of Plattsburgh (Clinton County) enacted an 18-month moratorium on new commercial cryptocurrency mining operations in 2018, citing energy cost impacts on residents due to low-cost hydropower being consumed by miners.
36019.notes: Moratorium expired but resulted in permanent permitting requirements
sources: Plattsburgh City Council, March 2018

53025.name: Grant County
53025.title: PUD Capacity Restrictions on High-Power Computing
53025.description: Grant County PUD placed restrictions on high-power computing operations including cryptocurrency mining due to transmission capacity constraints and cost-of-service concerns.
sources: Grant County PUD policy, 2018

53047.name: Okanogan County
53047.title: PUD Capacity Restrictions on High-Power Computing
53047.description: Okanogan County PUD imposed capacity-based restrictions on new high-power computing loads including cryptocurrency mining.
sources: Okanogan County PUD policy

51153.name: Prince William County
51153.title: Digital Gateway Area Restrictions
51153.description: Prince William County passed restrictions limiting data center density in the Digital Gateway area following sustained community opposition over environmental and land-use impacts.
sources: Prince William County Board of Supervisors, 2022

36061.name: New York County
36061.title: Local Law 97 Emissions Requirements
36061.description: NYC Local Law 97 (2019) imposes strict GHG emissions limits on large buildings including data centers. Facilities exceeding caps face substantial fines ($268/ton of CO2e over limit), effectively restricting data center expansion in NYC.
sources: NYC Local Law 97, 2019; NYC Mayor's Office of Climate and Sustainability

36047.name: Kings County
36047.title: NY State Crypto Mining Moratorium + Local Law 97
36047.description: New York State imposed a 2-year moratorium on new proof-of-work cryptocurrency mining operations using carbon-based power (signed Nov 2022). NYC's Local Law 97 also restricts data center emissions.
sources: NY Climate Law S6486C, 2022; NYC Local Law 97

36081.name: Queens County
36081.title: NY State Crypto Mining Moratorium + Local Law 97
36081.description: New York State's 2022 moratorium on proof-of-work cryptocurrency mining and NYC's Local Law 97 emissions limits affect data center operations in Queens.
sources: NY Climate Law S6486C, 2022; NYC Local Law 97

36005.name: Bronx County
36005.title: NY State Crypto Mining Moratorium + Local Law 97
36005.description: New York State's 2022 moratorium on proof-of-work cryptocurrency mining and NYC's Local Law 97 emissions limits affect data center operations in the Bronx.
sources: NY Climate Law S6486C, 2022; NYC Local Law 97

36085.name: Richmond County
36085.title: NYC Local Law 97 Emissions Requirements
36085.description: NYC's Local Law 97 emissions limits apply to all five boroughs including Staten Island, restricting large buildings including data centers.
sources: NYC Local Law 97, 2019

04013.name: Maricopa County
04013.title: Strict Water Use Requirements for Data Centers
04013.description: Arizona's Department of Water Resources requires 100-year assured water supply plans. Maricopa County data centers face increasingly strict water use requirements amid growing scarcity. Several data center projects have been delayed or redesigned.
sources: ADWR Water Shortage Regulations, 2022; AZ Senate Bill 1117

04021.name: Pinal County
04021.title: Water Use Restrictions
04021.description: Pinal County lost its groundwater supply designation in 2022, directly affecting planned data center projects that rely on evaporative cooling water. Multiple data center expansions have been impacted.
sources: ADWR Groundwater Supply Designation, 2022

30029.name: Flathead County
30029.title: Proposed High-Intensity Computing Restrictions
30029.description: Flathead County has proposed and discussed restrictions on cryptocurrency mining and high-intensity computing facilities following community concerns about noise and energy impact.
sources: Flathead County Commissioner discussions, 2019

06037.name: Los Angeles County
06037.title: CEQA Review, Emissions Standards, and AI Procurement Rules
06037.description: LA County requires extensive CEQA environmental review for new data centers. LA's Green New Deal targets zero-emission buildings. The City of LA has implemented data center energy efficiency standards. LA County has AI procurement review requirements for government systems.
sources: CEQA, LA Green New Deal (2019), LA County AI Procurement Policy

06075.name: San Francisco County
06075.title: CEQA Review, Building Emissions Rules, and AI Surveillance Restrictions
06075.description: San Francisco banned city use of facial recognition technology (2019). CEQA environmental review required for data centers. SF's building codes impose energy efficiency requirements on data centers.
sources: SF Ordinance 181184 (Surveillance Technology ban, 2019); CEQA

06085.name: Santa Clara County
06085.title: CEQA Review and Water Use Requirements
06085.description: Santa Clara County and the City of San Jose require extensive CEQA environmental review including water and energy impact assessments for new data centers. Silicon Valley water agencies have imposed stricter water use requirements.
sources: CEQA, San Jose Data Center Policy, Santa Clara Valley Water District rules

06001.name: Alameda County
06001.title: CEQA Review and AI Surveillance Restrictions
06001.description: The City of Oakland banned government use of facial recognition software (2019). Alameda County requires CEQA review for new data center development.
sources: Oakland Ordinance 13537 (2019); CEQA

17031.name: Cook County
17031.title: Illinois AI Video Interview Act
17031.description: Illinois' AI Video Interview Act (effective Jan 2020) requires employers to notify applicants when AI is used to analyze video interviews and obtain consent. One of the nation's first AI employment regulations.
sources: Illinois AI Video Interview Act, 820 ILCS 42 (2020)

08031.name: Denver County
08031.title: Colorado AI Act + Active Data Center Moratoriums
08031.description: Colorado's AI Act (SB 24-205, signed May 2024) imposes disclosure obligations, impact assessments, and consumer rights for high-risk AI systems — one of the nation's most comprehensive AI laws. Denver and neighboring jurisdictions have also implemented temporary moratoriums on new large-scale data center approvals amid rapid load growth and grid capacity concerns.
08031.notes: Colorado AI Act effective date: Feb 1, 2026. Data center moratoriums at the local level are distinct from state AI law.

sources.label: Colorado SB 24-205 — Artificial Intelligence Act (2024)

sources: Denver metro data center zoning review requirements (2023–2024)

48201.name: Harris County
48201.title: ERCOT Grid Curtailment and Demand Response Requirements
48201.description: Following Winter Storm Uri (Feb 2021), large power consumers including data centers in ERCOT territory must register for and participate in demand response programs. Data centers face potential curtailment during grid emergencies.
sources: ERCOT demand response programs; Texas SB 3 (2021)

48453.name: Travis County
48453.title: ERCOT Requirements and City of Austin AI Procurement Policy
48453.description: Travis County (Austin) data centers face ERCOT demand response requirements. The City of Austin adopted AI procurement guidelines requiring bias audits and impact assessments for government AI systems.
sources: ERCOT demand response; Austin AI Procurement Policy (2021)

48113.name: Dallas County
48113.title: ERCOT Grid Curtailment Requirements
48113.description: Dallas-area data centers are subject to ERCOT demand response requirements and potential curtailment during grid emergencies.
sources: ERCOT demand response programs; Texas SB 3 (2021)

48085.name: Collin County
48085.title: ERCOT Grid Curtailment Requirements
48085.description: Collin County (Allen/Plano/McKinney area) data centers are subject to ERCOT demand response and curtailment requirements.
sources: ERCOT demand response programs; Texas SB 3 (2021)

24031.name: Montgomery County
24031.title: Building Energy Performance Standards
24031.description: Montgomery County's Building Energy Performance Standards (BEPS) impose mandatory energy efficiency requirements on large commercial buildings including data centers as part of its Climate Action Plan.
sources: Montgomery County Climate Action Plan; BEPS regulations

24033.name: Prince George's County
24033.title: Data Center Zoning Review Requirements
24033.description: Prince George's County has implemented special exception zoning requirements for large data centers, requiring community input and environmental impact review.
sources: Prince George's County Zoning Ordinance

51059.name: Fairfax County
51059.title: Comprehensive Plan Restrictions — Data Center Growth Controls
51059.description: Fairfax County's 2022 Comprehensive Plan amendments impose significant data center restrictions: mandatory utility infrastructure capacity demonstrations, community impact mitigation plans, and firm limits on data center development in residential transition areas. A 2023 county board vote imposed further density caps amid community pressure over visual blight, noise, and grid strain.
51059.notes: County supervisors voted in 2023 to restrict new data center approvals in certain western Fairfax districts.

sources.label: Fairfax County Comprehensive Plan Amendment ZOAM 2022

sources: Fairfax County Board of Supervisors data center zoning amendments (2023)

53033.name: King County
53033.title: Environmental Review and AI Surveillance Restrictions
53033.description: King County banned government use of facial recognition technology (2021). New data center development requires SEPA (State Environmental Policy Act) review including energy and water impact assessment.
sources: King County Ordinance 19022 (2021); WA SEPA

27053.name: Hennepin County
27053.title: Minneapolis AI Surveillance Ban + Data Center Moratorium
27053.description: Minneapolis (Hennepin County) banned government use of facial recognition technology (2021) and requires AI impact assessments for government applications. Additionally, several Minneapolis-area municipalities have enacted or proposed moratoriums on large-scale data center development citing energy grid constraints and community opposition to large industrial footprints.

sources.label: Minneapolis City Council Resolution 2021

sources: Minneapolis-area data center development moratoriums (2023–2024)

25017.name: Middlesex County
25017.title: Cambridge AI Procurement Policy and MA AI Regulations
25017.description: Cambridge (home to major AI labs) has implemented AI procurement review policies. Massachusetts has proposed AI regulations affecting data center and AI deployments statewide.
sources: Cambridge AI Procurement Policy; MA proposed AI regulations

47157.name: Shelby County
47157.title: TVA Power Demand Scrutiny — xAI Colossus Supercomputer Hub
47157.description: Shelby County (Memphis) is home to xAI's Colossus supercomputer facility — the world's largest known GPU cluster as of 2024, drawing approximately 150 MW. The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) has implemented stricter scrutiny of large power loads in the Memphis area amid rapid demand growth, requiring enhanced interconnection review for new high-density computing facilities.
47157.notes: xAI Colossus Phase 1 launched Sep 2024 with 100,000 H100 GPUs; Phase 2 expansion ongoing.

sources.label: xAI Colossus announcement — Elon Musk / xAI, Sep 2024

sources: TVA Large Power Customer interconnection requirements
Tennessee Code Ann. § 67-6-206 data center sales tax exemption

04019.name: Pima County
04019.title: Water Use Requirements for Large Facilities
04019.description: Pima County (Tucson area) requires water use assessments for large facilities including data centers under Arizona's water management requirements.
sources: ADWR regulations; Tucson Water policies

09003.name: Hartford County
09003.title: Connecticut AI in Employment Act
09003.description: Connecticut passed SB 1103 (2022) requiring notice when AI tools are used in employment decisions, affecting businesses throughout Hartford County and the state.
sources: Connecticut SB 1103 (2022)

34035.name: Somerset County
34035.title: NJ Stormwater and Environmental Review Requirements
34035.description: New Jersey's updated stormwater and environmental review regulations impose additional requirements on large commercial developments including data centers. Somerset County is a major data center hub facing these requirements.
sources: NJ DEP Stormwater Rules; NJ Environmental Impact Statement requirements

37035.name: Catawba County
37035.title: Duke Energy Power Demand Management
37035.description: Catawba County is a major data center hub in NC. Duke Energy has implemented demand management and interconnection queue requirements for large new customers including data centers.
sources: Duke Energy interconnection requirements; NC Utilities Commission

13097.name: Douglas County
13097.title: Data Center Zoning Restrictions
13097.description: Douglas County commissioners voted against approving new data center developments citing concerns about land use, limited employment creation, and community character.
sources: Douglas County Board of Commissioners, 2023

56021.name: Laramie County
56021.title: Wyoming Digital Asset Regulations
56021.description: While Wyoming has been crypto-friendly at the state level, Laramie County has implemented local land use requirements for large digital asset mining and computing facilities.
sources: Wyoming Digital Asset regulations; Laramie County zoning

39049.name: Franklin County
39049.title: AEP Ohio Grid Capacity Requirements
39049.description: Franklin County (Columbus) is a major data center market. American Electric Power Ohio has implemented capacity queue management for large new data center interconnections amid rapid growth in demand.
sources: AEP Ohio interconnection queue management (2023)

42077.name: Lehigh County
42077.title: PPL/PJM Grid Interconnection Requirements
42077.description: Lehigh County (Allentown area) is a growing data center market. PPL Electric and PJM Interconnection have implemented stricter queue management and cost allocation rules for large new loads including data centers.

sources.label: PJM Interconnection queue reform FERC Order 2023

19153.name: Polk County
19153.title: Iowa 0% Data Center Tax Exemption — Des Moines Hyperscale Hub
19153.description: Polk County (Des Moines / West Des Moines) is Iowa's premier data center market. Microsoft operates a large campus in West Des Moines; Meta has a major facility in Altoona; Google, Aligned, and others have substantial presences. Iowa Code §423.3 eliminates sales and use tax on data center equipment and electricity for qualifying facilities, making Iowa one of the most data center-friendly states in the nation.

sources.label: Iowa Code § 423.3 — Data Center Exemptions
Microsoft data centers — West Des Moines, Iowa

sources: Meta Altoona, Iowa data center campus

19155.name: Pottawattamie County
19155.title: Iowa Data Center Exemption — Google Council Bluffs Campus
19155.description: Pottawattamie County (Council Bluffs) hosts one of Google's major data center campuses, operational since 2007. Iowa Code §423.3's 0% sales and use tax on data center equipment and electricity applies, along with low wind-energy power costs. Council Bluffs has become one of the Midwest's premier data center destinations.

sources.label: Iowa Code § 423.3 — Data Center Exemptions
Google Council Bluffs Data Center

39089.name: Licking County
39089.title: Ohio Data Center Tax Exemption — Amazon AWS New Albany Campus
39089.description: Licking County (New Albany) is home to one of Amazon AWS's largest data center complexes in the world, spanning millions of square feet across multiple campuses. Ohio's sales and use tax exemptions on data center equipment (Ohio R.C. 5739.02) and AEP Ohio's competitive power rates have made Licking County a global hyperscale destination.

sources.label: Ohio Development — Data Center Incentives
Amazon Web Services — Ohio data center region

sources: New Albany Data Center district — Licking County development

41065.name: Wasco County
41065.title: Oregon Enterprise Zone — Google The Dalles Campus
41065.description: Wasco County (The Dalles) hosts Google's first major owned data center, opened in 2006 and continuously expanded since. Oregon's enterprise zone property tax exemptions, zero sales tax, and cheap Columbia River hydropower make Wasco County one of the nation's most data center-friendly jurisdictions. Google's campus now spans hundreds of acres.

sources.label: Business Oregon — Data Center Tax Exemptions
Google The Dalles Data Center

41049.name: Morrow County
41049.title: Oregon Enterprise Zone — Amazon/Meta Boardman Campus
41049.description: Morrow County (Boardman) hosts major data center campuses from Amazon Web Services (since 2011), Meta/Facebook, and Google. Oregon's enterprise zone exemptions and Columbia River hydropower make Boardman one of the Pacific Northwest's most significant data center clusters.

sources.label: Business Oregon — Data Center Tax Exemptions

sources: Amazon AWS Boardman, OR data center; Meta Boardman data center campus

32003.name: Clark County
32003.title: Nevada Tax Abatements — Las Vegas Data Center Market
32003.description: Clark County (Las Vegas) is a major data center market anchored by Switch's PYRAMID campus and facilities from DataBank, Evoque, and other operators. Nevada's personal property tax abatements and sales tax exemptions for qualifying data centers (NRS §374.357) combined with no corporate income tax make Clark County a top Sunbelt data center destination.

sources.label: Nevada Governor's Office of Economic Development — Data Centers

sources: Switch PYRAMID campus, Las Vegas; NRS § 374.357 data center tax exemptions

48139.name: Ellis County
48139.title: Texas Chapter 312/313 Abatements — Google Midlothian Campus
48139.description: Ellis County (Midlothian) hosts a major Google data center campus serving the Dallas-Fort Worth metro area. Texas's historically generous Chapter 312/313 property tax abatements for large capital investments, combined with no state income tax and ERCOT grid access, have made Ellis County a significant data center hub.
48139.notes: Texas Chapter 313 (the school property tax abatement program) expired Dec 2022 but Chapter 312 abatements remain available through counties.

sources.label: Texas Economic Development — Data Center Incentives

sources: Google Midlothian, TX data center campus; Ellis County Chapter 312 abatements

55101.name: Racine County
55101.title: Wisconsin Incentives — Microsoft $3.3B AI Campus (Mount Pleasant)
55101.description: Racine County (Mount Pleasant) is the site of Microsoft's announced $3.3 billion AI data center campus, one of the largest tech infrastructure investments in Wisconsin's history. Microsoft announced the project in May 2024. Wisconsin's data center property tax exemption and Racine County economic development incentives apply.
55101.notes: Microsoft announced plans to build the campus in the Foxconn/Wisconsin Innovation Park site, which had been underutilized since Foxconn's scaled-back plans.

sources.label: Microsoft Wisconsin AI Data Center Announcement, May 2024
Wisconsin Economic Development Corporation

45003.name: Aiken County
45003.title: SC Data Center Incentives — Meta Aiken Campus
45003.description: Aiken County is the site of Meta's announced large-scale data center campus, leveraging South Carolina's industry-leading 20-year sales tax exemptions on equipment and 20-year property tax abatements for qualifying data center investments. Meta's investment follows Amazon AWS's presence in neighboring Berkeley County.

sources.label: South Carolina DOR — Data Center Tax Incentives

sources: Meta Aiken County, SC data center campus announcement

22083.name: Richland Parish
22083.title: Louisiana Incentives — Meta Louisiana Data Center
22083.description: Richland Parish hosts Meta's announced large data center campus in rural northeastern Louisiana, a project valued at over $800 million. Louisiana's Industrial Tax Exemption Program (ITEP) and competitive energy costs from Entergy Louisiana make it an emerging data center market. The facility leverages lower land and energy costs versus traditional coastal markets.
sources: Meta Richland Parish, Louisiana data center announcement (2023)

sources.label: Louisiana Economic Development — Industrial Tax Exemption Program

13121.name: Fulton County
13121.title: Georgia Data Center Tax Exemptions — Atlanta Metro Hub
13121.description: Fulton County (Atlanta) is a major US data center market and the core of Georgia's rapidly growing data center industry. Georgia's sales and use tax exemption on data center equipment (O.C.G.A. §48-8-3(82)) and the Atlanta area's fiber connectivity, power infrastructure, and business climate have attracted major hyperscale operators including Microsoft, Google, and multiple colocation providers.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions
Georgia Power — Data Center Solutions

48491.name: Williamson County
48491.title: Texas Incentives — Samsung Taylor Fab + Tech Investment Hub
48491.description: Williamson County (Taylor, Round Rock, Georgetown) is a fast-growing tech hub anchored by Samsung's $17B+ advanced semiconductor fab in Taylor (announced 2021) and major data center investment. Dell Technologies is headquartered in Round Rock. Texas's no-income-tax environment and historically generous Chapter 312 abatements make Williamson County a significant data infrastructure destination.

sources.label: Samsung Taylor, TX chip fab announcement (2021)
Texas Economic Development — Incentives

13135.name: Gwinnett County
13135.title: Georgia Data Center Tax Exemptions — Atlanta North Corridor Hub
13135.description: Gwinnett County (Suwanee, Lawrenceville, Duluth) is one of Metro Atlanta's fastest-growing data center submarkets. QTS Realty Trust, CyrusOne, and other major operators have significant facilities in the county. Georgia's data center sales tax exemptions and excellent fiber and power infrastructure underpin Gwinnett's growth.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

sources: QTS Suwanee, GA data center; CyrusOne Atlanta campus

18097.name: Marion County
18097.title: Indiana Data Center Tax Exemption — Indianapolis Metro Hub
18097.description: Marion County (Indianapolis) is a growing data center market anchored by Indiana's HEA 1450 (2014) 100% sales tax exemption on qualifying data center equipment and electricity. Indianapolis benefits from central US location, access to multiple fiber routes, and competitive power from AES Indiana. Multiple colocation and hyperscale operators have established facilities in the metro.

sources.label: Indiana Economic Development Corporation — Data Centers

sources: Indiana Code IC 6-2.5-5-59 data center equipment exemption (HEA 1450, 2014)

44007.name: Providence County
44007.title: Smithfield Permanent Data Center Ban
44007.description: The Town of Smithfield in Providence County enacted a permanent ban on large-scale data center development in 2023, making it one of the first municipalities in the US to outright prohibit data centers via zoning ordinance. The ban followed community opposition over energy grid impacts, water use, noise, and concerns that data centers provide few local jobs relative to their infrastructure burden.
44007.notes: Smithfield's ban is a permanent zoning prohibition, distinct from temporary moratoriums. It has been cited as a model by other municipalities nationwide.
sources: Smithfield, RI Town Council data center zoning prohibition (2023)

sources.label: Data Center Bans tracker — datacenterbans.com

37037.name: Chatham County
37037.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center Development Ban
37037.description: Chatham County enacted a moratorium on new large-scale data center developments in 2024, amid explosive growth proposals in the Research Triangle region. The moratorium reflects community concerns about grid capacity, water use, noise, and limited employment creation per acre of developed land.
sources: Chatham County Board of Commissioners data center moratorium (2024)

sources.label: NC data center moratorium wave coverage — Data Center Dynamics

37135.name: Orange County
37135.title: Active Restrictions — Data Center Development Moratorium
37135.description: Orange County (Chapel Hill/Carrboro) has enacted restrictions and temporary moratoriums on new data center developments. Community opposition in this progressive university county has focused on energy consumption, water use, and incompatibility with the county's climate commitments. Duke Energy transmission constraints in the area have amplified concerns.
sources: Orange County NC Board of Commissioners data center restrictions (2024)

sources.label: NC data center moratorium wave — Data Center Dynamics

37159.name: Rowan County
37159.title: Active Restrictions — Data Center Density Controls
37159.description: Rowan County (Salisbury) has enacted active restrictions on new large-scale data center development following rapid growth in applications. Duke Energy capacity constraints in the Piedmont region have contributed to a county-level pause on approvals while infrastructure and policy catch up.
sources: Rowan County NC Board of Commissioners data center restrictions (2024)
Duke Energy Carolinas interconnection queue constraints, Rowan County area

47001.name: Anderson County
47001.title: Active Moratorium — High-Intensity Computing Restrictions
47001.description: Anderson County (near Oak Ridge / Knoxville) enacted restrictions on new large-scale data centers and high-intensity computing following the xAI Colossus controversy and statewide concerns about TVA grid capacity. The county moratorium reflects concerns about power demand on the TVA transmission system serving eastern Tennessee.
sources: Anderson County TN data center moratorium (2024)
TVA large power customer capacity management, eastern TN

47065.name: Hamilton County
47065.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center and Crypto Mining Restrictions
47065.description: Hamilton County (Chattanooga) has enacted a moratorium on new large-scale data centers and cryptocurrency mining operations citing TVA grid capacity concerns and community opposition. Chattanooga's EPB fiber network had previously attracted significant data center interest.
sources: Hamilton County TN Commission data center moratorium (2024)
Tennessee data center restrictions wave, 2024

47179.name: Washington County
47179.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center Development Restrictions
47179.description: Washington County (Johnson City) enacted restrictions on new data center developments in 2024 amid the statewide wave of county-level data center moratoriums. Concerns center on TVA grid capacity, water use, and land use incompatibility with agricultural heritage.
sources: Washington County TN Commission data center moratorium (2024)
Tennessee county data center restriction wave, 2024

13063.name: Clayton County
13063.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center Development Ban
13063.description: Clayton County (south Atlanta suburbs) enacted a moratorium on new data center developments following high-profile rejection of proposed facilities. The county board cited concerns about land use, noise, limited employment relative to acreage consumed, and energy grid impacts.
sources: Clayton County GA Board of Commissioners data center moratorium (2023)

sources.label: Data Center Bans tracker

13285.name: Troup County
13285.title: Active Restrictions — Data Center Development Moratorium
13285.description: Troup County (LaGrange) enacted restrictions on new large-scale data center approvals following community opposition. Concerns over land use incompatibility, limited local employment, and grid and water demands drove the board's decision to pause new data center development.
sources: Troup County GA Board of Commissioners data center restrictions (2023)

32031.name: Washoe County
32031.title: Reno-Area Data Center Moratorium
32031.description: Washoe County / City of Reno area enacted a temporary moratorium on new large-scale data center development in 2023 following rapid growth that strained NV Energy grid capacity and prompted community concerns about water use in an arid region and noise from cooling equipment.
sources: Washoe County / Reno data center moratorium (2023)

sources.label: Data Center Bans tracker

26077.name: Kalamazoo County
26077.title: Proposed Moratorium — Data Center Development Restrictions
26077.description: Kalamazoo County has considered and partially implemented restrictions on new large-scale data center development following community opposition to proposed facilities. Concerns focus on energy demand on the Consumers Energy grid, water use, and land use impacts in agricultural and residential areas.
sources: Kalamazoo County MI proposed data center restrictions (2024)

sources.label: Data Center Bans tracker

37073.name: Gates County
37073.title: Proposed Data Center Restrictions
37073.description: Gates County NC has proposed zoning restrictions on large-scale data center developments as part of the broader wave of North Carolina county-level responses to rapid data center growth pressures in the state.
sources: Gates County NC proposed data center zoning restrictions (2024)

sources.label: NC data center moratorium coverage

37189.name: Watauga County
37189.title: Proposed Data Center Restrictions
37189.description: Watauga County (Boone, Appalachian State University area) has proposed restrictions on large-scale data center development citing incompatibility with the mountain tourism economy and limited grid capacity in the region.
sources: Watauga County NC proposed data center restrictions (2024)

37173.name: Swain County
37173.title: Proposed Data Center Restrictions
37173.description: Swain County (Bryson City, Great Smoky Mountains gateway) has proposed restrictions on large-scale data center development, citing grid constraints and incompatibility with rural and tourism land use.
sources: Swain County NC proposed data center restrictions (2024)

37115.name: Madison County
37115.title: Proposed Data Center Restrictions
37115.description: Madison County (Marshall) has proposed restrictions on large-scale data centers as part of the broader wave of western and rural NC counties seeking to limit high-power computing developments.
sources: Madison County NC proposed data center restrictions (2024)

08059.name: Jefferson County
08059.title: Denver Metro Data Center Environmental Review
08059.description: Jefferson County (Lakewood/Golden, Denver metro) has proposed enhanced environmental review requirements for large data center developments, driven by Colorado's Artificial Intelligence Act (SB 24-205, 2024) and broader community concerns about energy use and emissions from large computing facilities in the Denver metropolitan area.

sources.label: Colorado SB 24-205 — AI Act (2024)

sources: Jefferson County CO data center review requirements (2024)

47037.name: Davidson County
47037.title: Nashville Data Center Growth Controls
47037.description: Davidson County (Nashville) has proposed regulations on large-scale data centers following rapid growth in applications, strained TVA grid capacity, and community concerns. Nashville's rapid tech sector growth has accelerated data center demand while also raising opposition from community groups focused on land use.
sources: Davidson County TN Metro Nashville proposed data center regulations (2024)
TVA grid capacity management, Middle Tennessee

55025.name: Dane County
55025.title: Madison Area Data Center Environmental Review
55025.description: Dane County (Madison, University of Wisconsin) has proposed environmental review requirements for large data center developments as part of the county's sustainability commitments. Community opposition to proposed facilities has led to public hearings and planning commission reviews.
sources: Dane County WI data center environmental review proposals (2023–2024)

55071.name: Manitowoc County
55071.title: Proposed Data Center Restrictions
55071.description: Manitowoc County has considered restrictions on large-scale data center developments following community concerns about land use, limited employment generation, and energy demands on We Energies' grid.
sources: Manitowoc County WI proposed data center zoning restrictions (2024)

19061.name: Dubuque County
19061.title: Proposed Data Center Growth Management
19061.description: Dubuque County has discussed data center growth management requirements as data center developers have shown interest in the area. Despite Iowa's statewide pro-data-center tax exemptions, some Dubuque County communities have sought to review the impacts of large facilities on local infrastructure.
sources: Dubuque County IA data center development discussions (2024)

17113.name: McLean County
17113.title: Proposed Data Center Regulations
17113.description: McLean County (Bloomington-Normal) has proposed energy impact requirements for large-scale data center developments following interest from operators attracted to central Illinois's power grid and fiber connectivity. Community discussions focus on grid impacts and employment trade-offs.
sources: McLean County IL proposed data center energy impact requirements (2024)

49021.name: Iron County
49021.title: Data Center Moratorium — Water and Energy Constraints
49021.description: Iron County (Cedar City) has enacted restrictions and moratoriums on new large-scale data centers and cryptocurrency mining operations, citing acute water scarcity in Utah's arid southwest and grid capacity limitations on Rocky Mountain Power's network.
sources: Iron County UT data center restrictions (2023)

sources.label: Data Center Bans tracker

26161.name: Washtenaw County
26161.title: Ann Arbor Data Center Review Requirements
26161.description: Washtenaw County (Ann Arbor, University of Michigan) has proposed oversight requirements for large data center developments including environmental impact review. Ann Arbor's progressive city council has also considered AI governance policies for government systems, aligned with ongoing Michigan state-level AI discussions.
sources: Washtenaw County MI data center review requirements (2024)
Ann Arbor City Council AI governance discussions (2024)

13089.name: DeKalb County
13089.title: Stonecrest Data Center Moratorium
13089.description: DeKalb County and the City of Stonecrest (east Atlanta suburbs) enacted a moratorium on new large-scale data center approvals in 2024 after a surge of development applications. Community opposition focused on land use impacts, limited jobs per acre, and noise from cooling equipment in residential-adjacent areas.
13089.notes: The Stonecrest moratorium has attracted significant media attention as an example of suburban Atlanta communities pushing back on hyperscale data center growth.
sources: City of Stonecrest / DeKalb County GA data center moratorium (2024)

sources.label: Data Center Bans tracker

37183.name: Wake County
37183.title: Raleigh-Area Data Center Review Requirements
37183.description: Wake County (Raleigh) is one of the fastest-growing US data center markets, attracting major hyperscale investment. The county has implemented grid capacity review requirements for new large-scale data center applications following rapid Duke Energy interconnection queue growth. Community groups have also advocated for enhanced environmental review.
sources: Wake County NC data center growth management requirements (2023–2024)
Duke Energy Carolinas interconnection queue management, Wake County

37063.name: Durham County
37063.title: Proposed Data Center Review Requirements
37063.description: Durham County (Durham, Duke University Research Triangle) has proposed restrictions and enhanced review processes for large-scale data center development amid rapid growth interest in the Research Triangle. Community concerns focus on grid capacity, noise, and water use.
sources: Durham County NC proposed data center review requirements (2024)

37119.name: Mecklenburg County
37119.title: Charlotte-Area Data Center Review Requirements
37119.description: Mecklenburg County (Charlotte) has adopted data center impact review requirements following a rapid surge in development applications. Duke Energy transmission capacity constraints in the Charlotte metro area and community concerns about industrial land use have driven planning commission scrutiny of large data center proposals.
sources: Mecklenburg County NC data center impact review requirements (2023)
Duke Energy Carolinas interconnection queue constraints, Charlotte metro

37161.name: Rutherford County
37161.title: Duke Energy Power Demand Management
37161.description: Rutherford County (Forest City, Rutherfordton) data center developers face Duke Energy Carolinas interconnection queue management requirements as the broader NC data center boom strains the regional grid. Duke Energy has implemented capacity-based reviews for large new industrial loads including data centers throughout the western Piedmont region.
sources: Duke Energy Carolinas interconnection queue management (2023–2024)
NC Utilities Commission data center load review requirements

13217.name: Newton County
13217.title: Georgia Data Center Tax Exemptions — Google Covington Campus
13217.description: Newton County (Covington) hosts one of Google's major US data center campuses, opened in 2007 and continuously expanded. Georgia's sales and use tax exemptions on qualifying data center equipment and Georgia Power's competitive rates have made Newton County a significant data center location.

sources.label: Google Covington, Georgia Data Center
Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

47031.name: Coffee County
47031.title: Proposed Moratorium — High-Intensity Computing Restrictions
47031.description: Coffee County (Manchester) is among the Tennessee counties considering moratoriums on large data centers and cryptocurrency mining following the xAI Colossus controversy and statewide TVA capacity concerns in 2024.
sources: Coffee County TN proposed data center restrictions (2024)
Tennessee county data center moratorium wave, 2024
